metadata.title:
Locomotive Engineer Gives Damaging Testimony


In Defense Of Haywood.

pages.raw_text:
fered in the Vindicator case. He
absolutely denied anytrouble in the


Cripple Creek district from the


time the strike was declared until


the soldiers came in.


On direct examination Davis told


of his experience after being black


listed by the Mine Owners associa-


ton. He had travelled all over the


mining country under assumed


names in order to secure work. In


Arizona his employer found out his
name and he was discharged.


Orchard had testified that Davis
offered him 5000 to blow up the Vim
dicator mine. This Davis denied
absolutely and testified that he was
not near the mine the day the ex-
plosion occurred He said he had


offered his testimony to the defense


as soon as he read Orchards al
legged confession.


w. w. Rush, Of Denver, locomo-
tive engineer formerly employed by


the Florence & Cripple Creek rail-
road, was the next witness. He
was in charge of the engine which
figured in the spike pulling cases
for which Davis and other union


men were arrested and later &C
quilted.


Rush testified that Detective
Scott told him an attempt would be
made to wreck the train between
the Economic MI and Victor, and
to be very careful. He swore
Scott asked him If there was any
good place to wreck train between
the points named and he told him


of good place. Scott and Sterling


rode with him and dropped off at
the point designated.


Rush absolutely charged Detec-
tives Scott and Sterling, of the
Mine Owners association, with TAX
ing rail out of the track in order
to wreck the train and to throw
the blame on the striking miners.
Ed Boyce again took the stand
and went fully into the history of
the miners federation. He said his
wife had an interest in the rich


Hercules mine, sixteenth Of which
Orchard once owned. He began as
miner in March. 1888, in the
Occur d'Alenes, where he worked
years and wds Disseminated


against because lie belonged to the
union.


He was arrested and held at
Boise in Jail months in 1893.
Fourteen members OF the union,


he said, were sen. to Jail for viola


ton of an injunction of the federal


court restraining them from tres


passing on mining property.
Hawley, who IS now prosecuting


Haywood, carried the matter to the


supreme court for the federation.


The Western Federation of


Miners had It's Inception in the
Boise jail and Hawley advised them


how to organize The mine own


ers had organized In Helena in


1890. Boyce was still on the stand
when recess was taken.
